content:
4 min read
1 hour ago

--

A vague spec gets you a generic app.
A precise spec gets you the specific thing you actually wanted.
The difference isn’t the AI. It’s what you handed it.
✨ Start small, let the agent expand
Don’t sit down and try to write a 50-page document before you’ve built anything. You’ll over-engineer it, burn out, and end up with a spec the agent can’t actually use.
Instead: write a short goal statement and a few core requirements — a product brief. Hand it to the agent and ask it to draft a full spec from that. Review what it produces, correct anything wrong or hallucinated, and save it as SPEC.md.
That file is now your source of truth. For every task that follows, the agent reads it first.
✨ The six things your spec needs to cover
GitHub’s team analyzed over 2,500 agent configuration files to figure out what the effective ones had in common. Six areas came up every time:
1. Commands — not just tool names, full commands with flags. Write npm test, pytest -v, npm run build. The agent references these constantly, and vague tool names leave too much room for interpretation.
2. Testing — which framework you use, where test files live, what coverage looks like. If you don’t specify this, your agent will make something up.
